sections.heading: Modern-method satisfaction rose while overall demand barely changed
sections.body: Among currently married and sexually active women, total demand for family planning barely changed: 71.1 percent in 2025 and 70.7 percent in 2022. Unmet need was also almost the same, at 12.5 percent in 2025 compared with 12.4 percent in 2022. The bigger change was in modern-method satisfaction. The share of demand satisfied by modern methods rose from 59.1 percent to 62.5 percent, as met need for modern methods increased from 41.8 percent to 44.5 percent.

sections.heading: Regions differ more in whether demand is met than in demand itself
sections.body: Total demand ranged from 79.6 percent in Davao Region to 54.5 percent in BARMM, a gap of 25.1 percentage points. The gap was much wider for demand satisfied by all methods: 91.1 percent in Ilocos Region and 89.1 percent in Central Luzon, compared with 46.4 percent in BARMM. The biggest regional difference, then, is not only whether women want family planning, but whether that need is being met.

sections.heading: BARMM stands out because unmet need is higher than met need
sections.body: BARMM had the highest regional unmet need in 2025, at 29.2 percent. Its met need by all methods was 25.3 percent, meaning unmet need was larger than met need in the region. No other region followed that pattern. Even Central Visayas, which had the next-lowest met need by all methods at 47.8 percent, still had unmet need of 16.4 percent.

sections.heading: Similar demand can hide different levels of modern-method satisfaction
sections.body: MIMAROPA, Davao Region, and SOCCSKSARGEN had high shares of demand satisfied by modern methods, at 75.0 percent, 73.2 percent, and 71.9 percent. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had total demand of 68.2 percent, close to NCR at 69.2 percent and Bicol Region at 69.1 percent, but its modern-method satisfaction was 59.2 percent. That was below NCR at 60.1 percent and above Bicol Region at 55.6 percent, showing that regions with similar demand can still differ in how much of that demand is met with modern methods.

sections.heading: Several regions changed more sharply than the national trend
sections.body: Cagayan Valley had one of the clearest declines: demand satisfied by all methods fell from 90.3 percent in 2022 to 78.7 percent in 2025. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ also declined, from 79.8 percent to 73.0 percent for demand satisfied by all methods and from 68.2 percent to 59.2 percent for demand satisfied by modern methods. Davao Region moved the other way on total demand, rising from 72.3 percent to 79.6 percent, while demand satisfied by all methods remained high at 88.5 percent in 2025.

sections.heading: Women aged 15 to 19 have high demand, but much of it is unmet
sections.body: Women aged 15 to 19 had total demand of 81.1 percent in 2025, the highest among age groups. They also had the highest unmet need at 38.5 percent and the lowest demand satisfied by all methods at 52.5 percent. In contrast, women aged 35 to 39 had lower total demand at 74.8 percent but much higher satisfaction: 85.6 percent of demand was satisfied by all methods, and 65.0 percent was satisfied by modern methods.

sections.heading: Urban and rural areas differ most in modern-method satisfaction
sections.body: In 2025, urban and rural areas were close in total demand and unmet need. Total demand was 70.2 percent in urban areas and 72.2 percent in rural areas, while unmet need was 12.3 percent and 12.8 percent. The bigger difference was in demand satisfied by modern methods: 65.4 percent in rural areas compared with 60.0 percent in urban areas.

sections.heading: Family planning patterns do not rise neatly with education
sections.body: Women with Grades 11-12 education had the highest total demand at 79.3 percent and a relatively high unmet need of 19.1 percent. Women with Grades 7-10 education had lower total demand at 73.7 percent, but higher demand satisfied by all methods at 83.6 percent. College-educated women had lower total demand at 67.4 percent, while demand satisfied by all methods was 83.0 percent, close to the national level.

sections.heading: The clearest wealth differences are between the lowest and highest groups
sections.body: The lowest wealth quintile had total demand of 75.3 percent and unmet need of 16.3 percent, both above the national values. The highest wealth quintile had lower total demand at 64.2 percent and lower unmet need at 10.6 percent. Modern-method satisfaction was still higher in the lower quintiles: 64.5 percent in the lowest and 64.9 percent in the second quintile, compared with 58.7 percent in the highest quintile.

takeaways: From 2022 to 2025, national total demand and unmet need changed very little, but modern-method satisfaction improved.
The biggest story is the difference across regions, especially the gap between BARMM and the regions where most demand is satisfied.
Patterns by age, education, and wealth show that high demand does not always mean that demand is highly satisfied.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
